text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
We have now seen the position of the altar before the veil, the continuous morning and evening burning, the sacred incense, the fire from the sacrifice, the restriction against strange incense, and the crown of glory. Today we study two more features: the four horns of the altar and the square shape. These are among the most rich and layered features of the incense altar, and they point to Christ with remarkable precision.

At the four corners of the golden altar rose four projections called horns; made of the same piece as the altar itself, overlaid with the same pure gold, inseparable from the altar’s structure. These horns were not decorative.
They carried specific meaning and specific function. Exodus 30:10 says Aaron was to make atonement on these horns once a year with the blood of the sin offering. Leviticus 4:7 records the priest putting some of the blood on the horns of the altar. The horns bore the blood. The horns were the point at which atonement was declared and applied.

The horn in Scripture is a consistent symbol of strength and power. Deuteronomy 33:17 uses the horn as a picture of conquering strength.
Psalm 18:2 calls Yahweh “my horn of salvation” my strength of deliverance.
1 Samuel 2:1 gives Hannah’s song: “My horn is exalted in Yahweh.” And most significantly for the New Testament, Luke 1:69 records Zechariah’s prophecy at the birth of John the Baptist: “He has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of His servant David.” The horn of salvation is Christ. The horn on the altar declares that the intercession rising from this altar is not weak, not uncertain, not easily overcome. It is carried by strength. The strength of the risen, living Christ who always lives to intercede.

The blood applied to the horns on the Day of Atonement speaks of what Hebrews 9:11-14 describes: “But when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come … He entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of His own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption. For if the blood of goats and bulls … sanctifies for the purification of the flesh, how much more will the blood of Christ … purify our conscience from dead works to serve the living God.” The horns of the incense altar bore the blood of the sin offering every year. Christ entered the true Most Holy Place once, with His own blood, and secured an eternal atonement. The horns bear the blood. The blood makes the intercession acceptable. Our prayers rise to God not because of our goodness but because the horns of this altar are covered in the blood of Christ.

And there were four horns one at each of the four corners. The number four in Scripture consistently points to universality and completeness the four corners of the earth, the four winds, the four directions. The four horns of the incense altar declare that the atonement of Christ and the intercession He makes are not limited to one nation, one tribe, one people, or one generation. They reach in every direction. The blood on the horns is symbolic of the power of the blood of Christ to save all, to the four corners of the earth. 1 Timothy 2:5-6 says: “For there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself as a ransom for ALL.” The gospel goes to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8). The house of prayer is for ALL nations (Mark 11:17). The four horns of the golden altar declare that the intercession of Christ extends to every corner of the world without distinction or limit.

The golden altar was one cubit long and one cubit wide perfectly square.
The same measurement in every direction. This is significant because a square has no dominant side. Every face is equal. Every direction is the same. There is no angle at which the altar is longer, shorter, wider, or narrower. It is perfectly balanced in all directions.

This speaks of the perfect, balanced, complete ministry of Christ. Hebrews 7:26 describes the High Priest who fits our need as “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens.” Every quality in perfect measure. Not strong in holiness but weak in compassion.
Not compassionate but careless about righteousness. Not merciful but indifferent to truth. Every dimension of His character and His ministry is equal, complete, and perfectly balanced like a square. His intercession for the weak is just as strong as His intercession for the mature. His mercy toward the new believer is just as full as His mercy toward the long-serving one. His attention to the prayer whispered in private is just as complete as His hearing of the prayer offered by a congregation. The altar is square. Chris is perfect.

The one cubit square symbolises that the intercession of Christ reaches to the four corners of the earth without distinction or interruption it is without limits. And the two cubits in height taller than both the Ark and the Table of Showbread speak of the prayers of the saints that have overcome the world and reached the heavens because of Christ’s intercession. The prayers we offer are not kept below the ceiling by our own inadequacy. They are lifted to the height of the heavens by the one whose intercession is higher than the heavens themselves (Hebrews 7:26). The square altar that stands two cubits tall is preaching: this intercession reaches everywhere, and it reaches all the way to the throne.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, how great is Your goodness, which You have stored up for those who fear You (Psalm 31:19). You placed four horns on the altar of incense and covered them with the blood of atonement, declaring that access to Your presence is not for one tribe or one nation but for all who come through the blood. We thank You for the universal reach of the intercession of Christ that the four corners of the earth are held in the strength of His mediation, that no person is too far from the altar, that no language, no background, no history places any person beyond the reach of the horn of salvation. And we thank You for the perfect, square completeness of His ministry that His intercession is the same for every one of us, balanced, full, and without partiality. May we carry this truth on our shoulders today. In Christ’s name, amen.
